coredata.dc:title: Upconversion graphene quantum dots incorporation in performance enhancement of p-i-n perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Irannejad, Neda
Rezaei, Behzad
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dc:description: Photon upconversion with the potential to reduce spectral mismatch losses is a promising approach in improving solar cell performance. Utilizing the synergistic effect of quantum dots (QDs) along with upconversion (UC) properties in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) is an efficient strategy to extend the absorption spectrum from visible into near-infrared (NIR) range, which leads to an explosive evolution in improving solar cell performance. Herein, upconversion graphene quantum dots (UC GQDs, with the ~4.5 nm particle size) are used to design an improved PSC. UC GQDs are incorporated into p-i-n PSCs by adopting interlayers, next to the hole transport layer (HTL), perovskite, and rear (Au) electrode, as HTL/UC GQD/PSK, PSK/ UC GQD/ETL, ETL/UC GQD/Au structure respectively. The designed CuI/PSK/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC, by expanding the absorption range from near-infrared (NIF) photons into a visible region, increasing the light-harvesting capability following GQDs, and the suppression of charge recombination processes following a passive effect have shown an effective role in improving p-i-n PSCs performance. These subtly PSCs resulted in a high-power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 19.79% (18.06 ± 1.73) compared to (15.07%, 14.97 ± 0.10) pristine PSCs. The attractive hydrophobic property of the UC GQD has led to an increase in the long-term stability of the resulting solar cell under harsh environmental conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic design of p-i-n perovskite solar cells.
PV parameters distributions for CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au devices with different UC GQDs interlayers: HTL/UC GQD/PSK, PSK/ UC GQD/ETL, ETL/UC GQD/Au.

(a) TEM image of UC GQDs, the inset shows the HRTEM image and DLS result with the size distribution of UC GQDs; (b) X-ray diffraction data for the as-synthesized UC GQDs; and (c) FT-IR spectrum, and (d) Raman spectrum of UC GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of UC GQDs in aqueous solution, and inserted Tauc plot, (b) Typical UC GQDs PL spectra, (c) UC GQDs UCPL spectra under different excitation wavelengths. (d) UC GQDs PLE spectrum in emission wavelengths of 450 nm in the absence and presence of a cut-off filter of 400 nm, respectively; the inset shows fluorescent and upconversion emission intensity at excitation wavelengths of 330 and 740 nm. (e) The PL plot integrated intensity, excited by the selected light of 330 and 740 nm for normal PL and UCPL as a function of temperature. (f) A schematic illustration of UC GQDs normal and upconverted PL mechanisms, and Cyclic Voltammetry curve.

(a) A schematic illustration of Perovskite/UC GQD/ETL-based PSCs with the absorption spectra of the perovskite, and UC GQD, and UC GQD emission spectrum. PV performance of the CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au. (b) J–V curves of high performing PSCs measured in forward and reverse scans (scan rate 200 mV/s) under simulated sun irradiation (AM 1.5) and inserted histogram of the number of cells as a function of PCE (at least 40 cells for each type of device) fitted with a normal distribution. (c) External Quantum Efficiency (EQE) spectra and integrated short-circuit current density (Jsc) of the corresponding devices. (d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and inserted equivalent circuit. (e) Normalized absorption spectra. (f) Normalized photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and (g) XRD spectra.

(a) and (d) Top-view and (c and e) cross-sectional view of field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM in backscatter mode) images, (c and f) AFM topography images of CuI/perovskite, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films.

Contact-angle test (CA, with water droplets) as a function of the loading time of (a) CuI/perovskite, and (c) CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films. Optical images of the perovskite films (without encapsulation) as a function of humidity exposure time in 90 ± 5% RH. The corresponding XRD spectra of the (b) CuI/perovskite, and (d) CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films.
(a) 20–30% RH ambient stability test and (b) Thermal stability test (at 85 °C) of the CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au devices without encapsulation.
Photovoltaic characteristics of CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au devices with different UC GQDs interlayers. Performances of the best efficiencies are presented with average values and standard deviation.
Recombination resistance (Rrec) of CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au devices obtained from Nyquist plots.

head.ce:title: Upconversion graphene quantum dots incorporation in performance enhancement of p-i-n perovskite solar cells

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photon upconversion with the potential to reduce spectral mismatch losses is a promising approach in improving solar cell performance. Utilizing the synergistic effect of quantum dots (QDs) along with upconversion (UC) properties in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) is an efficient strategy to extend the absorption spectrum from visible into near-infrared (NIR) range, which leads to an explosive evolution in improving solar cell performance. Herein, upconversion graphene quantum dots (UC GQDs, with the ~4.5 nm particle size) are used to design an improved PSC. UC GQDs are incorporated into p-i-n PSCs by adopting interlayers, next to the hole transport layer (HTL), perovskite, and rear (Au) electrode, as HTL/UC GQD/PSK, PSK/ UC GQD/ETL, ETL/UC GQD/Au structure respectively. The designed CuI/PSK/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC, by expanding the absorption range from near-infrared (NIF) photons into a visible region, increasing the light-harvesting capability following GQDs, and the suppression of charge recombination processes following a passive effect have shown an effective role in improving p-i-n PSCs performance. These subtly PSCs resulted in a high-power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 19.79% (18.06 ± 1.73) compared to (15.07%, 14.97 ± 0.10) pristine PSCs. The attractive hydrophobic property of the UC GQD has led to an increase in the long-term stability of the resulting solar cell under harsh environmental conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Unprecedented advances in outstanding perovskite photovoltaic performances have led to a dramatic increase in perovskite solar cell's power conversion efficiency (PCE) from ~3% to over ~25% (~28% in tandem architecture) [1–3]. Achieving this milestone in perovskite solar cells is owed to the brilliant properties of the perovskite adsorbent layer with a typical AMX3 crystal structure (A: Cs+, Rb+, CH3NH3 +, NH2CH=NH2 +, CH3CH2NH3 + monovalent cations; M: Pb2+, Sn2+; and X: Cl–, Br–or I– halides) [4–6]. The synergistic role of high electron mobility (~ 800 cm2. Vs−1), charge carrier emission length (which exceeding more than 1 µm), the absorption coefficient (more than 105 cm−1 following s-p antibonding coupling), the exciton binding energy (~less than 10 meV) along with high photoluminescent quantum efficiency (as ~70%), optimal bandgap with the adjustability, the low recombination rate of perovskite structure have led to significant improvements in perovskite solar cells [7–11].

Despite the rapid growth of perovskite solar cell (PSC) efficiency (~25% for the small active area 0.1 cm2), promoting them from the laboratory to the factory is still challenging [12–14]. Achieving PSC commercial applications depends on two fundamental principles: (i) improving PSCs performance by device enlargement, and (ii) increasing their long-term stability under harsh environmental conditions [15–18]. Intrinsic restrictions on PSC design, lack of uniform coverage of functional layers [19], grain boundaries and bulk defects [20], ion migrations [21,22], and recombination processes along with the external factors such as light, temperature [23], and humidity [24,25], are parameters affecting the PSCs performance and stability [26]. According to the Shockley–Queisser limit, photovoltaic systems are only able to absorb photons whose energy is greater than the semiconductor bandgap energy. Photons with energy lower than the photovoltaic systems bandgap energy are wasted [27–29]. These drawbacks make it impossible to use the maximum capacity of the standard solar spectrum (AM 1.5 G, 280–2500 nm, 0.5–4.4 eV). One potential approach to overcome the Shockley–Queisser limit and non-absorbed photon loss in perovskite solar cells is the employment of structures with upconversion properties [30–32]. Upconverting (or upconversion) structures with the ability to convert long-wavelength (typically 800–1000 nm) to shorter wavelengths (mostly visible range) through an anti-Stokes process, have played a vital role in improving solar cells performance [33,34]. The upconversion phenomenon is based on a nonlinear optical process during the successive absorption of two or more low-energy NIR photons. This successive absorption leads to the excitation of electrons to an intermediate state and eventually raises them to higher energy levels. The light emission at higher energies (wavelengths between 300 and 800 nm) results from the relaxation created following such excitation states [35]. The three main types of upconversion (UC) materials are lanthanide-based, transition metal-based, and molecular UC structures [36,37]. Lanthanide-based materials are one of the most common compounds with upconversion properties. Rare-earth ions with full 5 s and 5p orbitals have 4–4 f and 4 f-5d electronic transmissions. A major challenge in using Lanthanide-based with upconversion properties in PSCs is the poor compatibility between these compounds (containing long-chain alkyl molecules such as oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OM) as ligands) with perovskite precursors [38]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a unique generation of nanostructures that can show upconversion properties [32]. Upconversion GQDs (UC GQDs) with high optical stability along with low phonon energy, wide light absorption, long fluorescence life, high physical and chemical stability, good adaptation of the emission spectrum to the perovskite absorption spectrum, high quantum efficiency, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity have revolutionized photovoltaic systems [39–41].

Herein, we exploited a simple approach to synthesize graphene quantum dots with upconversion properties (UC GQDs). The role of synthesized UP GQDs in improving the performance of p-i-n perovskite solar cells has been evaluated as an interlayer. The results showed that the use of UC GQDs as an interlayer (perovskite/UC GQDs/electron transfer layer), with the ability to convert low-energy NIR photons into high-energy visible photons, have played an effective role in improving the PSCs performance (PCE: 19.79% (18.06 ± 1.73)). In this fascinating study, the (p-i-n) CuI/Perovskite/UP GQDs/PCBM/Au PSC structure by showing the hydrophobic properties in the presence of high humidity (90 ± 5% RH), and good long-term stability in ambient (20%−30% RH, 25 °C) and thermal (85 °C) conditions promises a huge revolution in the acquisition of PSCs with a commercialization approach.

Upconversion graphene quantum dots were synthesized by using citric acid monohydrate (CA) and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as precursor materials. In the perovskite solar cells fabrication process, fluorine-doped tin oxide ions (FTO)/glass sheets (8 Ω cm−2, 14 mm × 14 mm, Solaronix Co.) were used as substrate. Copper iodide (CuI, 99.9%, Sigma-Aldrich Co.) and phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM, 99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich Co.) were used as hole transport layer (HTL) and electron transport layer (ETL) respectively. Lead iodide (II) (PbI2, 99%), lead (II) Bromide (PbBr2, 99%), methylammonium bromide (MABr, ≥99.5%), formamidinium iodide (FAI, ≥ 99.5), cesium iodide (CsI, 99.9%), were used as precursor materials for designing “mixed perovskite” absorption layer with Cs0.05(MA0.17FA0.83)0.95Pb(I0⋅83Br0.17)3 structure. All materials such as Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, 98%) and solvents include chlorobenzene (anhydrous CB, 99.8%), N, N-dimethylformamide (anhydrous DMF, 99.8%), dimethyl sulfoxide (anhydrous DMSO, 99.9%), acetonitrile (anhydrous AC, 99.9%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company and used as provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Upconversion graphene quantum dots (UC GQDs) synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize UC GQDs, a clear mixture of 1.0 mmol CA, 3.0 mmol TEPA, and 5.0 ml DMF was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave which heated for 6 h at 180 °C. The as-prepared brown solution was centrifuged several times at 9000 rpm [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UC GQDs-incorporated p-i-n perovskite solar cells fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The p-i-n perovskite solar cells in this work were fabricated with the main FTO/CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au configuration. As shown in Fig. 1, to achieve the best efficiency and long-term stability, the role of UC GQDs as different types of interlayers was evaluated. Before each procedure, the FTO/glass substrates were cleaned in an ultrasonic bath for three 10-minute steps with deionized water, acetone, and 2-propanol, respectively. The HTL was prepared by spin-coating the CuI acetonitrile solution (0.05 M) on the FTO substrate at 4000 rpm for 30 s, which then annealing at 100 °C for 10 min [43]. The perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving PbI2 (1.1 M), PbBr2 (0.22 M), FAI (1.0 M), MABr (0.2 M), and CsI (0.05 M) in anhydrous DMF: DMSO (4:1) mixed solvent. To prepare Cs0.05(MA0.17FA0.83)0.95Pb(I0⋅83Br0.17)3 perovskite absorber layer, the precursor solution was spin-coated on FTO/CuI substrate via two continuous steps: (i) the 1000 rpm for 10 s (at 250 rpm s−1 acceleration rate), and (ii) 6000 rpm for 20 s (at 2000 rpm s−1 acceleration rate) which the CB antisolvent was dripped onto the spinning substrate surface at 13 s of the second step [44]. The ETL was prepared by spin-coating the PCBM chlorobenzene solution (0.25 mol) on FTO/CuI/perovskite substrate at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, 50 nm gold film as back contact was thermally evaporated using the DST3-A coater system (Nanostructured Coatings Co., Iran). To design modified PSCs, saturated UC GQDs in chlorobenzene solution was prepared. To obtain a stable saturated solution, different concentrations of UC GQDs (10 mg ml−1, 15 mg ml−1, and 25 mg ml−1, 35 mg ml−1 in CB) were prepared, and their stabilities at ambient temperature were evaluated. According to the obtained results, the 25 mg ml−1 saturated UC GQDs in CB solution was more stable (~ 180 min). Each of the interlayers (HTL/UC GQD/PSK, PSK/ UC GQD/ETL, ETL/UC GQD/Au with the ~60 nm thickness) were prepared by spin-coating the prepared solution (25 mg ml−1 saturated UC GQDs in CB) at 2000 rpm for 30 s (Fig. 1) which then annealing at 100 °C for 10 min.

To obtain useful information on the distribution, shape, and UC GQDs particle sizes, the Philips CM30 300 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) at 300 kV, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Malvern ZEN 3600) were used. Valuable UC GQDs structural information was recorded using X-ray diffraction (XRD) by D8-Advance Bruker using copper emission (Kα, at 35 mA and 40 kV, 2θ from 10° to 80°), and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (using KBr pellets with a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer). The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded by AvaSpec-2048-USB2 (200–900 nm), and the JASCO FP-750 fluorometer respectively to prove the UC GQDs upconversion property.

Perovskite solar cell morphologies were characterized by a Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM: HITACHI S 4160) and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM: Bruker Nanos instrument, Germany). Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) was achieved using a Philips XL30. To characterize the perovskite structure, the Grazing Incident X-ray Diffraction (GIXRD) patterns were recorded by Asenware AW/XDM 300 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å, under operation condition of 40 kV and 30 mA). The perovskite layer absorbance spectra (UV–Vis) were measured with an AvaSpec-2048-USB2 spectrophotometer. The water contact angle measurements were performed by using Contact Angle Measurement System (CA-500A) to investigate the hydrophobicity of fabricated perovskite layers.

To evaluate designed perovskite solar cells performance, current density-voltage characteristic curves in both forward and backward direction (J-V), were obtained by Biologic SP-300 potentiostat under simulated sunlight illumination AM 1.5 G, 100 mW cm−2. Photon-to-Current conversion Efficiency (IPCE, measured by IRASOL’s IPCE-020 with the tunable LED light source), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS, measured by Eco-Chemie Autolab PGSTAT 302 N electrochemical workstation controlled with NOVA software) were performed to obtain deeper and broader information about the PSCs performance mechanism.

The stability of designed perovskite solar cells was performed in all cases without any encapsulation. The self-healing ability was conducted under humid conditions (90 ± 5% RH). Long-term stability was investigated by placing nonencapsulated perovskite solar cells in ambient (20%−30% RH, 25 °C) and thermal (85 °C) conditions.
Given the UC GQDs effective role in NIR light-harvesting, the idea of incorporating UC GQDs interlayer in designing p-i-n PSCs to increase the PSCs performance was evaluated.

The device configurations depend on the UC GQDs interlayer deposition are as follows: HTL/PSK/ETL/Au (pristine PSC), HTL/UC GQD/PSK, PSK/ UC GQD/ETL, ETL/UC GQD/Au. Photovoltaic (PV) characteristics obtained from the evaluation of J-V curves ( Fig. 2, and Table 1) indicate the effective role of UC GQDs interlayer position in the performance of the resulting p-i-n PSCs. The results confirm the better performance of PSK/UC GQD/ETL-based PSCs compared to pristine PSCs. Due to the significant improvement in p-i-n PSCs resulting from the use of PSK/UC GQD/ETL structure and consideration of maximum PCE (19.79%), high open-circuit voltage (Voc, 1.15), suitable short-circuit current density (Jsc, 20.99), and fill factor (FF, 82%), further studies have focused on this structure. To gain deeper insight into the source of performance improvement of PSK/UC GQD/ETL-based PSCs, the photochemical properties of the UC GQDs adsorbent layer were evaluated.

CA, due to its unique molecular structure, is a great choice for the synthesis of high fluorescent GQD structures with upconversion capability [45–47]. In the synthesizing process of UC GQDs, CA, and TEPA act as a source of carbon, and nitrogen respectively. The fast, simple, and efficient synthesis approach, resulted in UC GQDs yielding up to 86% of the CA source [42]. Fig. 3a, shows the TEM, HRTEM, DLS, XRD, FT-IR, and Raman analysis of UC GQDs. The TEM image (Fig. 3a) indicates the spherical shape formation of UC GQDs nanoparticles. DLS analysis evaluation indicates that the particle size distribution follows the Gaussian distribution, which ~4.0–4.5 nm (in the range of ~2.0–5.5 nm) is the most probable average size. HRTEM images reveal the UC GQDs crystalline nature with a 0.35 nm lattice space, corresponding to (002) graphite [48–50]. To further explore the UC GQDs structure, the X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was evaluated. The diffraction peak observed at 2θ~ 24° in Fig. 3b, is related to graphite plates (002) [51–53], which confirmed the successful UC GQD structure synthesis. The XRD results are in full agreement with the HRTEM results. The FT-IR spectrum contains useful information regarding the UC GQDs structure (Fig. 3c). The wideband in ~3000–3500 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The appearance of a peak located at ~2740 cm−1 (C-H stretching vibration) is following the non-carbonization of some precursors during the synthesis process. The C-N stretching peak at ~1050 cm−1 indicates the successful placement of nitrogen atoms in the graphitic lattice. The CO and CC vibration peaks have appeared in the ~1000–1500 cm−1 [45,54].

Raman spectroscopy was used to phase evaluation of UC GQDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that the as-synthesized UC GQDs were composed of sp2 aromatic and aromatic C–N species derived by TEPA molecules along with abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the GQDs surfaces. Raman spectroscopy noticed that the G-band and D-band arose from the in-plane of sp2 carbons and sp3-hybridized carbons, respectively. Therefore, the intensity ratio of ID/IG (~1.0) is a reliable indicator with which to verify changes from sp2 carbons into defective forms of sp3 configuration in graphene [55].

The UV-Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra contain useful information about the successful synthesis and optical properties of as-prepared UC GQDs [56,57]. Fig. 4a shows the UV-Vis spectrum with three characteristic absorption bands at 240, 285, 330 nm with a long tail absorption sequence up to 900 nm (NIR range). The absorption band observed at 240 nm belongs to the graphitic sp2 domain in the sp3 C matrix (π-π * transitions). The two absorption peaks observed at 285 and 330 nm are derived from CO and conjugated C-N/CN bands (n-π * transitions) [58,59].

One of the most effective methods for determining the optical bandgap energy, Eg, is the use of Tauc’s plots obtained by UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy [60]. The bandgap energy (Eg) can be calculated by αhν = A( hν – Eg)n. In this case, the optical bandgap energy can be calculated by extrapolating the tangent line of the (αhν)n plot drown vs. hν, where the proportionality constant, photon energy, the frequency of vibration and absorption coefficient were indicated by the A, hν, ν and α symbols. As shown in the inserted Tuac plot in Fig. 4a, the synthesized UC GQD structure has a bandgap of 2.8 (eV).

The non-dependence of QD's photoluminescence properties on excitation, following the diversity of different functional groups on the surface, confirms the successful synthesis of GQDs (Fig. 4b) [61–63]. Under normal light, UC GQD shows a clear and colorless solution. The fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 4b confirmed the maximum fluorescence emission at ~450 nm due to the UC GQDs excitation at 330 nm with the ~70 nm full-width at half-maximum (FWHM). The upconversion property is a unique feature in QDs [64,65]. GQDs Upconversion capability was evaluated in Fig. 4c. In this study, to eliminate the monochromatic second harmonics generation, a 400 nm cut-off filter was used. By UC GQDs excitation in the 600–850 nm range, a strong upconverted emission appears at 450 nm. UC GQDs excitation at 740 nm has led to the highest intensity of upconversion. The spectra obtained in the evaluation of 600–850 nm excitation wavelength, show a slight redshift. The photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE) study is a valuable method for evaluating the UC GQDs excitation process [66,67]. Fig. 4d shows the results of the PLE study. At 450 nm emission wavelength, two characteristic excitation peaks at 285 and 330 nm have appeared. Excited-state energy trapping along the electronic surface states resulted in appearing strong emission spectra with the excitation range of 280–350 [68,69]. Weak emissions observed in the form of two peaks at ~510 and 620 nm have belonged to Normal PLE double wavelengths [62]. The high-intensity photoluminescence emission at 330 nm is in good agreement with the maximum fluorescence peak that appeared in the PL spectrum with 330 nm excitation. After eliminating the monochromator interference using a 400 nm cut-off filter, the UCPL spectrum of the UC GQDs was recorded. The appearance of a characteristic peak at 740 nm following the 450 nm emission wavelength is a confirmation of the GQDs upconversion properties. The inserted images in Fig. 4d show the UC GQDs fluorescence and upconversion emission at the 330 and 740 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. Investigating PL and UCPL intensity dependence is an effective way to prove the upconversion property [70,71]. By evaluating the temperature effect on the normal PL and UCPL intensity (Fig. 4e), it can be observed that the increase in temperature leads to a decrease in the normal PL intensity while it has no clear effect on the UCPL intensity. The observed behavior is owed to the intrinsic PL and UCPL process mechanism differences [72,73]. As the probability of non-radiative transitions increases with increasing temperature, the normal PL intensity decreases. In the UCPL mechanism, the additional energy received as the temperature rises provides the transition energy needed, which justifies the lack of significant change in UCPL intensity. A schematic diagram of the PL and UCPL processes is shown in Fig. 4 f. The PL process follows an effective transition from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) (Fig. 4d (I)) while the upconversion PL process follows an anti-Stokes transition (Fig. 4d (II)). The synergistic role of UC GQDs upconversion and downconversion indicates its unique performance in UV, visible, and NIR spectrum of sunlight.

To determine the position of the HOMO and LUMO levels in the UC GQD structure, it is possible to use the cyclic voltammetry method. The position of energy levels in this field is calculated using the empirical Bredas et all. equations [60]:
(1) E(HOMO)=-[4·65 V-Eox(onset)]
(2) E(LUMO)=-[4·65 V-Ered(onset)]
(3) Eg=LUMO–HOMO

In this method, Ferrocene is used as a reference to determine energy levels, so that the value of ferrocene is − 4.65 eV. To record the CV curves, prepared films with a surface area of 1 × 1 cm2 were used as a working electrode. The Ag/AgCl electrode (as organic reference electrode) and Pt electrode (auxiliary electrode) were used for the three electrodes system. For this purpose, a solution of 0.1 M of TBAPF6 in anhydrous acetonitrile was used under a nitrogen atmosphere at a scan rate of 20 mV/s. The CV curve recorded is shown in Fig. 4 f. As estimated from Fig. 4 f, the HOMO and LUMO levels obtained from the CV curve (Eg = 2.8 eV) and bandgap obtained from it are in good agreement with the bandgap obtained from Tauc’s plots.

Fig. 5a shows the p-i-n PSC configuration and the UC GQDs principles to harvest broadband NIR light and spectral conversion to visible light which can be absorbed by the perovskite layer. The absorption in the perovskite absorber layer is limited to harvest the AM 1.5 G ultraviolet-visible spectral range so that the absorption capacity is radically reduced at the edge of longer wavelength absorption. Utilizing the synergistic role of UC GQDs upconversion and downconversion properties has significantly enhanced the luminescence emission in the visible region, which exactly falls within the perovskite absorption spectrum. The more charge carriers production and additional photocurrent improvement resulted in a significant PSC photovoltaic performance enhancement.

The J-V curves obtained from the backward-forward scan for the CuI/Perovskite/PCBM/Au, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au are shown in Fig. 5b. In the UC GQDs incorporation, the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC has no serious hysteresis effects (with hysteresis factor: 0.105%) compared to the pristine PSC structure (with hysteresis factor: 0.273%) [74]. The J-V hysteresis behavior principle is still debated. However, operational factors related to the solar cell performance (such as precondition, scan rate and direction, and voltage range,) and internal influencing factors (such as PSC architecture, unbalanced carrier transport, dynamic trapping and de-trapping processes, ion migration, and ferroelectric polarization) are among the important parameters affecting the hysteresis phenomenon [75–77]. Improved the visible emission in the GQDs incorporation with upconversion and downconversion capability is in good agreement with the perovskite absorption region. Enhancing the light-harvesting and increasing the excited electron number lead to the enrichment of deep-localized trapping levels, which reduced the PSC recombination tendency. Suppression of charge carrier recombination in grain boundaries is evidence of a significant hysteresis reduction.

The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra are shown in Fig. 5c. The results clearly show that the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC, in the range of high wavelengths (400–800 nm), has a higher external quantum efficiency compared to pristine PSC. Utilizing the GQD with upconversion and downconversion properties as an interlayer between the perovskite and the ETL leads to a significant improvement in visible range absorption. The integration current density value obtained from IPCE curves for the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au (21.03 mA cm−2) matches very well with the Jsc value obtained from the corresponding J-V measurement (Table 1). The increase in Jsc in the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au PSC (21.03 mA cm−2) compared to pristine PSC (20.87 mA cm−2) is owed to the GQDs upconversion effect which inhibited interfacial recombination and enhanced charge carriers excitation and collection [78].

To gain a deeper insight into the charge transfer dynamics, carrier recombination, and internal resistances in the PSC, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz-1.0 MHz was used. The semicircles appearing at high and middle frequencies indicate the charge transportation resistance (Rct) between the HTL and the perovskite layer, and the recombination (Rrec) resistance respectively [79,80].

Based on the Nyquist curves (Fig. 5d and Table 2), the PSCs designed based on the UC GQDs interlayer (CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC structure) have a higher recombination resistance (17708 Ω) compared to the pristine PSC structure (6493 Ω). Utilizing maximum solar spectrum in the presence GQD with upconversion and downconversion properties along with improving charge carrier collection, can lead to improved PSC performance. The superior charge separation and transportation processes in the presence of UC GQDs interlayer, play an effective role in suppressing charge recombination and lead to an increase in Voc [81].

The normalized UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD and CuI/Perovskite films are shown in Fig. 5e. The perovskite is only able to absorb incident photons whose energy is equal to or greater than the perovskite bandgap. Low-energy photons that the perovskite is unable to harvest are absorbed by the UC GQDs (Fig. 5e) and then transferred as high-energy photons that can be absorbed by the perovskite layer, which contributes to the additional generation of photocurrents [38]. These observations are in full confirmation with the IPCE results. Under these conditions, the maximum harvest of the solar spectrum is achieved. To determine the charge carrier non-radiative-recombination properties, photoluminescence spectra (PL) were investigated in Fig. 5 f. The pristine perovskite has an emission peak at 750 nm. The Perovskite/UC GQD film exhibits a higher PL peak intensity than that of the pristine perovskite, which indicates the reduction in the defect trap states and charge carrier non-radiative recombinant processes. The synergistic role of absorption enhancement by incorporation of the GQD with upconversion and downconversion properties, along with reducing grain boundaries and bulk defects, leads to the successful suppression of recombination processes [82,83].

The low angle grazing incidence X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was evaluated for pristine perovskite and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films [84]. Fig. 5 g shows the corresponding XRD pattern. The main diffraction peaks located the 2θ ~ 14°, 20°, 23°, 24.5°, 26°, 28.5°, and 32.5°are belong to (110), (112), (211), (202), (220), and (310) pristine perovskite crystal structure planes, respectively. As can be conducted, the presence of the UC GQD layer had no destructive effect on the perovskite layer.

The surface morphologies of CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD and CuI/Perovskite films were evaluated using FESEM and AFM. As reflected in Figs. 6a and 6b, the formed pinholes at the surface of the pristine perovskite structure are visible. The Perovskite/UC GQD structure shows less defined grain boundaries and crystal borders (Figs. 6d and 6e). This indicates the UC GQD effective role in creating a more uniform surface and improving the grain and crystalline boundaries of the perovskite structure. The improved morphology can be attributed to the presence of UC GQD with an average size of 4.5 nm, which are well dispersed on the surface of the perovskite film and provide a more uniform structure. The role of CB solvent in slight perovskite surface desolvation and its recrystallization can also strengthen the crystal growth mechanism. The more continuous and uniform surface coverage formation in the presence of UC GQD as the interlayer between perovskite and ETL can facilitate the process of charge transport in the p-i-n PSCs [30,85].

The top-view morphologies and root-mean-square roughness (RMS) of the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD and CuI/Perovskite films were characterized using the AFM technique. According to the obtained PV results, in the presence of UC GQD compared to pristine perovskite, FF is significantly improved (FF: 82% and 69.77% for the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au, and CuI/perovskite/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSCs respectively). This observation indicates the effect of UC GQD on the surface morphology of the perovskite film. As shown in Figs. 6c and 6 f, the Perovskite/UC GQD films, with a smoother and more uniform surface, has an RMS = 49.9 nm which is much lower than the pristine perovskite films (RMS = 93.0 nm). The increased roughness is equivalent to an increase in the number of holes that are responsible for recombination and FF reduction. The more uniform morphology obtained in the presence of UC GQD, with the ability to faster dissociation of photogenerated electrons, and promotes efficient electron transport, plays an effective role in improving FF [86,87].

The perovskite layer's ability to withstand water penetration and its hydrophobicity is an important factor in the stability of PSCs [27]. In Fig. 7 the water droplet contacts angle (CA) on the surface of the CuI/perovskite, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films are evaluated. The CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD film, with a much higher contact angle (Fig. 7c, CA: 119.8°) has good potential to tolerate moisture, compared to CuI/perovskite film (Fig. 7a, CA: 98.5°).

Healing processes of the CuI/perovskite, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD films were investigated by XRD analysis. Fig. 7a and b show that the pristine perovskite film is not able to recover after 60 s being kept away from the moisture source and remains yellow. This color change (from black to yellow) indicates the degradation of the perovskite structure which is confirmed by obtained XRD spectra. According to the XRD patterns, the pristine perovskite film is degraded after being kept away from water vapor, which the PbI2 peak has appeared (2θ ~ 12°) [88,89]. As reflected in Fig. 7c and d, Perovskite/UC GQD film can retain its initial value in ~60 s and return to black immediately after being kept away from water vapor. The XRD results, confirm the ability of Perovskite/UC GQD film to spontaneously heal itself. The absence of any obvious changes in the Perovskite/UC GQD film XRD pattern, confirms the ability of this structure to successfully heal itself.

Once the perovskite film is exposed to water vapor, water adsorption on the surface causes the hydrolysis of perovskite into PbI2, and the perovskite grains begin to disintegrate away from each other. Leakage of MA+ and FA+ ions through channels leads to irreversible degradation of the perovskite structure. The perovskite film remains yellow after degradation. In CuI/Perovskite/UP GQDs/PCBM/Au PSC due to the presence of the hydrophobic UP GQDs with an average size of 4.5 nm as an interlayer, the grain boundaries are well attached. Suppression of MA+ and FA+ ions through the interconnected grain boundaries, along with the suppression of trapped charge migration toward organic cation, prevent deprotonation of the organic cations. The formation of anchors between organic cations in the nearest octahedral sites of the perovskite structure, instead of escaping them, significantly increases the stability of the PSCs. Following the removal of water vapor, the perovskite film degradation reaction in the backward direction, leading to the formation of the perovskite crystal structure again.

The synergistic role of hydrophobicity along with the reduction of grain boundaries to create more uniform perovskite film has led to an increase in its long-term stability which provides technical support for the commercialization process of PSCs.

The PSC's long-term stability is an influential factor in the commercialization process of this type of solar cell [90]. To gain a deep insight into long-term stability, the performance of nonencapsulated CuI/Perovskite/PCBM/Au, and CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSCs under 20%− 30% RH in ambient conditions ( Fig. 8a) and at 85 °C (Fig. 8b) was evaluated.

PSCs designed based on CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au PSC structure retain ~96% of their initial efficiency after 600 h, while a pristine PSC is only able to maintain ~40% of its initial efficiency during this period. Examination of the PSC's long-term stability under the thermal condition (at 85 °C) also confirms the better performance of the CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au PSCs structure. After 300 h, CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au PSC retains ~64% of its initial efficiency, while the pristine PSC, can only maintain ~11% of its initial efficiency at the same time. The more uniform morphology obtained in the presence of UC GQD interlayer, with the ability to faster dissociation of photogenerated electrons, and promotes efficient electron transport, along with reduced grain boundaries where the volatile ions can readily escape plays an effective role in improving long-term stability [91,92].

Here, to reduce the PSC's spectral mismatch losses, UC GQDs were evaluated as an interlayer between perovskite and ETL in p-i-n PSCs. These novel photon energy UC GQDs (with the ~4.5 nm size and up to 86% QY) were prepared by a simple and fast method which have the potential ability to convert broadband infrared light to visible perovskite benefiting light. The systematic studies indicated that PCE improvements were conducted by the incorporation of GQDs with upconversion and downconversion properties as CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au p-i-n PSC structure. The synergistic role of maximum light-harvesting along with multidimensional defect passivity and surface modification for surpassing non-radiative charge carrier's recombination in the presence of UC GQD interlayer led to an amazing improvement in perovskite solar cell performance. The results reflect the design of CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au PSC with a 19.79% maximum PCE without hysteresis compared to pristine PSC with 15.07 maximum PCE has improved its long-term operational stability against heat and humidity without any encapsulation. The hydrophobicity of the designed Perovskite/UC GQD structure prevents perovskite hydrolysis in outdoor conditions. CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au-based PSCs exhibit superior stability which retained ~96% of its initial PCE after 600 h stored in 20–30% RH ambient conditions, while the pristine perovskite-based PSCs only retained ~40% of its original PCE. Increasing the thermal stability of CuI/Perovskite/UC GQD/PCBM/Au-based PSCs at 85 °C over 300 h preserves ~64% of their original PCE (pristine perovskite-based PSCs, ~11.0%) without any encapsulation. Utilizing unique properties of GQDs with upconversion and downconversion properties in the effective charge carrier's separation, accelerating the charge transfer process following the reduction of internal resistance, as well as the hydrophobicity tendency, and passivating recombination process is a promising approach to facilitate PSCs commercialization.

Neda Irannejad: Conceptualization, Synthesis, Characterization of compounds & Data analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-original draft.
